column1: # Principle: Types are first-class. Type literals push as values; the
# test type bindings. None is the unit type, spelled `none`; its sole
# inhabitant IS the value `none`. The atom `null` is reserved for JSON
# This spec consolidates everything about types in aqleng:
#   1. Type-name words push type-literal values (Integer, String, …).
#      Type names are globally unique so the renderer prints just the
#   2. `none` parses to a None type literal. Rendering: 'none'.
#   3. `null` parses to an Atom('null') — the JSON-null value, NOT a
#   4. `type NAME body` installs a type binding (NAME starts [A-Z]).
#      Body can be a type literal, a record-shape map (`{x:Integer}`),
#   6. `typeof v` returns a Type literal — the type of v as a value.
#      For a concrete value, returns its exact VType (`typeof 5 →
#      Integer`). For a type literal, returns its metatype (`typeof
#      Integer → ScalarType`). For `none`, returns None itself.
#   7. `v is T` returns Boolean — does v satisfy type T?
#   8. Type literals are rejected at every concrete-payload sig slot
#      (anything other than TAny / metatype / matching TNone).
# Type literals are the language's value-level handle on its types —
# they're how you spell 'the type Integer' or 'the type List'. Every
# reflective / generic operation (predicates, type equality, type
# unions, dispatch) builds on them. typeof + is together let user code
# branch on type at run time without resorting to ad-hoc string
# comparisons; the Type-typed return of typeof keeps the result
# composable (typeof's output IS a value usable by `is`).
# This is the aqleng (engine-kernel) subset. The richer type words
# (record, table, object, make, guard, inspect, base, convert, …) are
# layered on top in production aql; their semantics depend on parser /
# native-mod features that are intentionally out of aqleng scope.
#   addq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   subq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] - args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   concatq  — [String String] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (string concat)
#   firstq   — [List] forward-prec — first element or None for empty list
#   lengthq  — [List] forward-prec — list length as Integer
# A bare unquoted word that matches a type name is recognised by the
# engine (typeNames table in eng/go/engine.go) and resolves to a
# type-literal Value: VType = the named Type, Data = nil. The renderer
# prints just the leaf of the type path because type names are
# Leaf rendering — every type literal renders as its leaf.
# Family roots (Scalar / Node / Object) and the metatype root (Type)
# are themselves type-name words that resolve to type literals.

column3: String subtype: type of every non-empty string
String subtype: unique inhabitant is the empty string

# 11i. Typed list / map carrying both elements AND a child constraint
# `[{x:1} :{x:Integer} {x:2}]` parses to a typed-list value whose
# ChildTypeInfo retains both the literal elements and the child
# constraint. `{a:{x:1} :{x:Integer} b:{x:2}}` is the map analogue.
# `is` against a child-typed type validates each retained element /
# entry-value against the child constraint. The element retention is
# what makes `[v0 :T v1]` more useful than the bare `[:T]` form: the
# value carries the elements forward so list / map / first / length

column1: # `enum [a b c]` builds a Disjunct from the listed values. Words in
# the list become Atoms (so users don't need `quote` on each member).
# The enum is typically named via `type Color enum [...]` and queried
# A child-typed list `enum [: T v0 v1 ...]` validates each value
# against T at construction; a non-matching value triggers a type
# error before the disjunct is built.
type Color enum [ red green blue ] quote red is Color
type Color enum [ red green blue ] quote green is Color
type Color enum [ red green blue ] quote pink is Color
type Codes enum [: Integer 200 404 500 ] 200 is Codes

# function-shape constraint with NO body. It is the type-only
# counterpart to `fn` (which always carries a body and produces a
# An FnSig is not callable; its only role is as a type. The intended
# instantiation pattern is `type NAME fnsig […]` to name the shape,

# before binding. (Direct value matching of FnSig vs Function uses
# the typed-def path; the spec runner's local fixtures don't include
# the unification machinery here, so this section sticks to what the
# Bare fnsig produces an FnSig type literal.

column1: # `fnsig` is strict about its outer-list grammar: each pair MUST be
# `[in] [out]` (length is non-zero multiple of 2). Adding a body
# triple is an error — that's `fn`'s territory.

column3: mod-3 (with body) is rejected; use fn for that

column1: # Once an enum is registered as a type via `type Color enum […]`,
# Color is an FnSig-style disjunct constraint. The typed-def form
# `def x:Color body` validates the body against Color: only members
# of the disjunct succeed, anything else triggers a type_error.
# This is AQL's analogue of 'instantiating an enum value' in other
# languages. Survey of the surrounding design space:
#   Rust          Color::Red          variant path on the enum type
#   Swift         Color.red           case access on the enum type
#   TypeScript    Color.Red           value of an enum type
#   Haskell       Red                 data constructor of the sum type
#                                     bind time; x then resolves to
#                                     Atom(red) and `x is Color` is true
# AQL's twist is that the 'instantiated value' is just the underlying
# Atom — no nominal wrapper distinguishes `Atom(red)` from
# `Color::Red`. The Color membership check happens at def-time; once
# bound, x is an Atom that *happens* to satisfy Color.
# Atom-valued enum — typed binding admits members.
type Color enum [ red green blue ] def x:Color quote red x
column3: red is a Color member; bind succeeds

column1: type Color enum [ red green blue ] def x:Color quote green x
type Color enum [ red green blue ] def x:Color quote blue x
type Color enum [ red green blue ] def x:Color quote pink
column3: pink is not in Color's alternatives

column1: type Color enum [ red green blue ] def x:Color quote red x is Color
column3: the bound value still satisfies Color

column1: # Integer-valued enum — typed binding accepts matching literals.

type Codes enum [: Integer 200 404 500 ] def s:Codes 404 s
type Codes enum [: Integer 200 404 500 ] def s:Codes 201
# String-valued enum — same pattern, String members.
type Mode enum [: String 'off' 'on' 'auto' ] def m:Mode 'on' m
type Mode enum [: String 'off' 'on' 'auto' ] def m:Mode 'fast'

# Every concrete-payload sig slot rejects type literals (Data == nil).
# Type literals are still legitimately accepted at: TAny slots,
# metatype slots (Type/*), and the TNone slot for `none`.
# tagq has [Integer]→'specific' and [Any]→'any'. Every type literal
# Arithmetic and string handlers reject type literals.
